frameworks.name: dimensional models of affect
frameworks.one_line: affective quality decomposed into multiple dimensions inferred from neural data

frameworks.name: valence-arousal 2D model
frameworks.one_line: classical two-dimensional account positing valence and arousal as sufficient

frameworks.name: positivity-negativity 2D model
frameworks.one_line: alternative two-dimensional account using bivariate positivity and negativity

frameworks.name: representational structure analysis (fMRI)
frameworks.one_line: neural representation used to adjudicate competing affective dimensionalities

core_question: What is the dimensional structure of affective representation in the brain, and is a 2D model sufficient?
key_assumptions: affective experience can be described by a small set of dimensions
neural activity reflects an underlying affective representational structure
fMRI patterns can discriminate among candidate dimensional models
manipulations: candidate dimensionality of the affective space (2D vs 4D)
choice of dimensions (valence/arousal vs positivity/negativity vs all four)
mapping of affective dimensions onto brain regions
key_findings: a four-dimensional model is optimal for examining the affective data
valence is represented as a gradient along the fusiform gyrus
distinct bilateral amygdala sub-regions track arousal versus negativity
interpretations: the standard 2D affective models obscure structure revealed by a 4D analysis
anatomically organized neural codes support multiple affective dimensions jointly
